html:
Why this syntax atlas exists

The public demo should prove that a personal site can read serious Org, not only three TODO rows. This source intentionally mixes prose, tables, links, drawers, timestamps, source blocks, lists, footnotes, and planning metadata.

The key rule is simple: TypeScript may arrange the experience, but native Org syntax remains the source of truth.fnnative-org

What the reader should notice

  • Blog headings still become articles when tagged :blog:.

  • Italic, bold, underline, strike-through, verbatim, and code should survive rendering.

  • Links can point to Org mode, stable records, and attachment-like evidence.

  • Footnotes, tables, drawers, and source blocks should not flatten into plain text.

Knowledge is useful only when it can be revisited, re-ranked, and acted on.

fnnative-org In this demo that means property drawers, planning lines,

headline tags, source blocks, and rendered HTML all come through the WASM parser boundary instead of a TypeScript approximation.

Syntax rendering checklist

This article doubles as an agenda item. The same heading must appear in the Blog reader, the Notes list, and the Agenda workbench.

Inline and block syntax

  1. Inline emphasis: bold, italic, underline, deleted, verbatim, inline-code.

  2. Nested list:

    • Parser-owned facts

      • TODO keyword

      • priority cookie

      • planning line

    • Frontend-owned presentation

      • source cards

      • article library

      • agenda panels

  3. Checkbox state:

    • render checked task

    • render partial task

    • render open task 

This example block should remain monospaced.
It is useful for command transcripts and raw Org fragments.

type AgendaSurface = {
  source: "org";
  parser: "wasm";
  frontend: "typescript";
};

console.log("native Org first", {
  parser: "orgize",
  watch: "rspack",
});

A compact table

Syntax familyExampleFrontend risk
PlanningDEADLINE / SCHEDULEDhidden from agenda grouping
Properties:AREA:, :EFFORT:, :ID:collapsed into plain text
Linksweb, id, attachmentlost labels or wrong target
Blocksquote, example, sourcebroken spacing or overflow
Tablesorg tableunreadable in Zen mode

Records

Property drawer coverage

This record exists to verify drawer preservation and display. It should expose multiple key-value pairs without requiring the user to inspect raw Org.

Agent memory promotion contract

The frontend should show why this fact is current: TODO state, schedule, stable identity, habit cadence, links, and lifecycle notes all remain visible. See property drawer coverage for the source record that seeded this contract.

Superseded memory lane sketch

This older layout is intentionally retained as historical evidence so the UI can explain stale and superseded candidates instead of hiding them.

Archived memory import experiment

Archived records should stay inspectable while remaining suppressed for active agent decisions.

Attachment and ID link follow-up

The body carries both an attachment link and an ID link: syntax map and rendering checklist.

Agenda

Audit table overflow in Zen reading

Tables, source blocks, and long links must stay readable on desktop and mobile.

Add visual fixtures for attachment cards

The demo currently uses attachment links as semantic placeholders. A later slice can add real images under public assets.

Capture baseline syntax corpus

The first version of this corpus was added after the UI stopped showing development-only compiler state.
